author: @michaelschauperl172
text: When Robert did show the electro magnetic properties of that ink in one of his videos. I thought that i would love to see if a thread coverd in that ink could be used instead of copper wire in a spoul to produce electricity and vice versa Imagine a electric car motor without the need of copper in its components.

author: @timothygorman7528
text: Hey Luke - What about taking little sticker circle dots, you know, like "label" stickers for a yard sale or the "stars" your teacher stuck on things in grade school or something like that; coating/painting the dots with the conductive ink and sticking them to the finger tips you want to use on the glove/mitten.  We live in such a "convenience" oriented society, I could see selling "finger tip stickers that will work on your phone with gloves" as a real product.  Sure everyone would wear them out or get them wet or something, but it's really simple to reach into your wallet or purse and grab a strip of stickers to replace it with.  Would that work or must it be "sewn" into for the capacitive nature of it to work?

replies.author: @timothygorman7528
replies.text: Ok, so reading in the comments,  I see the "thread must touch your finger inside the glove to work."  That being said, yeah, sewing some thread through a finger tip is brilliant :)

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text: Seeing that Canon lens cap in one of the shots reminds my of another trick one can use for gloves.  Operating a camera in really cold conditions (or even in a warm environment if you’re a snowflake making a fashion statement) where gloves are a necessity, it is difficult to press the various buttons on it and other equipment. It is possible to purchase – at great expense–  Nordic type gloves made for photographers, where one can slip back the index finger and thumb sleeves revealing one’s naked digits. However, with these there is a real risk in subzero temperatures of one’s flesh ‘instantly’ sticking to any bare metal surface one inadvertently touches (don’t ask me how I know – OK!). Also at circa minus 40 temperatures one needs mittens over the tops of gloves as well, making buttons even more of a challenge.  Instead, just stick onto  the gloves or mittens suitably sized self adhesive rubber or felt feet. The type one can buy on amazon ect.,  for putting on the bottom of PC cases and what not.  They need to be those that have a small diameter which suit the size of the buttons.   [ An aside:  If these FWG videos are being filmed on a Canon do you use a Gonine ACK-E6  kit or something similar so you can power it directly from the mains without having to keep charging the darn battery]

replies.author: @michaelcorbidge7914
replies.text: So the battery and chemical  film had no problems  at low  temperature?

replies.author: @Nuts-Bolts
replies.text:
@michaelcorbidge7914 Film is OK at -40 (remember to place film rolls/SSD cards/camera/lenses/laptops  in sealed containers or plastic bags before bringing  indoors to prevent condensation forming on them). [For those readers’ who can’t fathom how their laptops failed from ‘water damage’ even though they have never spilt any liquid on them ever — now they know].  A bonus with digital cameras at cold temperatures is that they generate less thermal noise, which is helpful. 

With batteries, one needs spares, kept warm inside jacket, to swap as the battery in use become too cold. Regardless of what some manufactures claim, their capacity falls off a cliff as the temperature drops. Before lithium batteries came along, much equipment used nickel cadmium batteries.  These are the only dry cell technology (that I’m aware of) which continue to work down to – 40, so are still used today where possible.  The old mercury button batteries for the internal light meters also lost capacity to deliver any useful power.

author: @AlaskanInsights
text: has anyone tried one of them conductive ink circuit pens and just draw new finger tips on your glove ? I suspect that would also work.

replies.author: @dgpreston5593
replies.text:
To work best, the conductive thread needs to be exposed both within and without the glove's fingertip.

You touch the thread inside, the thread touches the phone outside, job done. 
I haven't tried just ink on the outside.
Let us know if it works! Good idea to try.
